The fixture's "clean" code adds get_item_by_name to src/demo/db/repository.py and manually instruments it: a function-local `from opentelemetry import trace`, `tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)`, `with tracer.start_as_current_span("db.repository.get_item_by_name") as span`, and `span.set_attribute(...)`. This is the OPPOSITE of the template's real convention. (1) Tracing model: the template instruments the DB layer via AUTO-instrumentation — `SQLAlchemyInstrumentor().instrument(engine=engine)` in src/framework_cli/template/src/{{package_name}}/observability/datastores.py.jinja — so the actual repository functions in src/framework_cli/template/src/{{package_name}}/db/repository.py (list_items, create_item) contain ZERO manual spans and rely on the instrumentor to trace every query. The only manual start_as_current_span anywhere in the template is in a TEST (tests/unit/test_logging.py.jinja), never in app/repo source. The fixture thus models a manual-span pattern the codebase deliberately avoids. (2) Import/guard convention: OTel-touching modules either import at module top with no lazy need (logging_config.py) or gate imports behind settings.otel_enabled / TYPE_CHECKING (datastores.py.jinja, tracing.py.jinja); the fixture's bare in-function import with no otel_enabled guard matches neither, and the real repo module imports no OTel at all. (3) Typing: the fixture uses a quoted forward-ref `-> "Item | None"` without `from __future__ import annotations`, whereas db/repository.py uses a bare unquoted `list[Item]` and the modules that do need deferred annotations open with `from __future__ import annotations`. Net: as an observability-db consistency oracle this "good" example teaches that hand-wrapping a repository query in a tracer span is idiomatic, when the codebase's actual clean pattern is a span-free repository function backed by SQLAlchemyInstrumentor — a weak/misleading oracle. (batteries: [] also gives it no battery context.)
suggested_pattern: Re-author the clean example to mirror db/repository.py's real shape: a plain SQLAlchemy repository function with NO manual OpenTelemetry span, e.g. `def get_item_by_name(session: Session, name: str) -> Item | None:` returning `session.scalars(select(Item).where(Item.name == name)).first()` — bare (unquoted) return annotation, module-level imports only, and no in-function `from opentelemetry import trace`. The "observed query" is realistic precisely because SQLAlchemyInstrumentor (observability/datastores.py.jinja) auto-traces it; the good fixture should demonstrate that a correctly-written repo function needs no hand-rolled tracing, matching list_items/create_item.

(4) redis.json and redis_alerts.yml are faithful subsets of the template's own redis.json / redis_alerts.yml.jinja: same uid/title/tags/schemaVersion=39, same up{job=\"redis\"} expr, same alert shape (up == 0, for: 5m, severity: warning). Only cosmetic deltas exist and are themselves consistent with template conventions: redis:7 vs template redis:7-alpine, and the fixture's dashboard/alert are slightly leaner than the template's richer versions (template adds a RedisHighMemory alert + extra panels) — neither breaks the pattern.

The fixture extends the real template file src/{{package_name}}/routes/items.py and correctly imports get_logger/correlation_id_var (both real, from logging_config.py), uses structlog event-name + kwargs logging, and 204 status — surface-level these match. But its CENTRAL clean pattern is an anti-pattern relative to the codebase's actual convention. The template injects correlation_id AUTOMATICALLY via the add_correlation_id structlog processor (logging_config.py:21-25); the contextvar is SET once at the request boundary (middleware/observability.py:38) and READ only by that processor. Confirmed by grep: NO log call anywhere in the template manually passes correlation_id= as a kwarg. The error paths that would be the natural place to do so deliberately omit it — observability.py:48 _log.error('request_failed', method/path/duration_ms) and errors.py:49 _log.error('unhandled_exception', error_type/method/path) both rely on the processor. The fixture does the opposite, manually threading correlation_id=correlation_id_var.get() into delete_item_not_found and delete_item_failed log calls — redundant double-injection the real code never does. Secondary divergences: module-level import + _log = get_logger() are placed mid-file AFTER the function (lines 11-13) instead of at module top as in circuit_breaker.py:13-16 / retry.py:22-26 / errors.py:20-27; and a function-local 'from fastapi import HTTPException' (line 19) where items.py:3 imports fastapi symbols at module top. So it is a weak consistency oracle: it would train the observability reviewer to bless manual per-call correlation-id injection, which contradicts the template's repeated automatic-processor idiom.
suggested_pattern: Re-author the delete route to follow the template's automatic-correlation idiom: import get_logger at module top (top of items.py with the other imports), define _log = get_logger() at module level above the routes, and emit structlog events WITHOUT a manual correlation_id kwarg — e.g. _log.error('delete_item_failed', item_id=item_id) and _log.warning('delete_item_not_found', item_id=item_id), letting the add_correlation_id processor (logging_config.py) inject it, exactly as observability.py:48 and errors.py:49 do. Import HTTPException at module top, not inside the function. Drop the correlation_id_var import entirely since the processor owns it.

reason: The fixture's central "clean" signal — a route that manually opens a span and sets attributes (`tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)`, `with tracer.start_as_current_span("items.archive") as span: span.set_attribute("item.id", item_id)`) — is a pattern the template NEVER uses. A grep of the whole template `src/` for `start_as_current_span`/`get_tracer`/`set_attribute` returns zero hits. The template's tracing model is AUTO-instrumentation in `src/{{package_name}}/observability/tracing.py.jinja` (`FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app)`, `CeleryInstrumentor().instrument()`), with span/trace context flowing into logs automatically via `logging_config.py::add_trace_context`. Every real route/handler (`routes/items.py`, webhooks `handler.py`/`inbox.py`, `routes/graphql.py.jinja`, `tasks/retention.py.jinja`) does NO manual span work — they simply call `get_logger().info("event_name", **kv)`. Two further divergences from the target file `routes/items.py`: (1) the fixture puts `from opentelemetry import trace`, `from ..logging_config import get_logger`, and `from fastapi import HTTPException` INSIDE the function body, whereas items.py and circuit_breaker.py import these at module top (the template's only deliberate local imports are in tracing.py/datastores.py to avoid loading the OTel SDK in disabled processes, not a route convention); (2) the fixture returns `-> dict` with an ad-hoc `{"archived": True,...}`, while the template's route convention is precise typing plus a Pydantic response contract (`get_items(...) -> list[Item]` decorated with `response_model=list[ItemRead]`). What IS consistent: the `get_logger()` import path and structlog event-key logging style, and `SessionDep`/`router.post`/`session.get(Item,...)`. But the load-bearing "instrumented" pattern is exactly the one the codebase avoids, making it a misleading consistency oracle for the observability agent.
suggested_pattern: Re-author the clean route to mirror the template's real convention: rely on FastAPI auto-instrumentation (no manual `get_tracer`/`start_as_current_span`/`set_attribute`), import `from ..logging_config import get_logger`, `from fastapi import HTTPException` at module top (alongside the existing top-level imports in routes/items.py), emit structured events with `get_logger().info("archive_item_completed", item_id=item_id)` like webhooks `handler.py`/`inbox.py` and `tasks/retention.py.jinja`, and give the route a precise return type with a Pydantic response schema + `response_model=` decorator the way `get_items(...) -> list[Item]` / `ItemRead` do — i.e. an observability-clean route is one that is well-logged via structlog and auto-traced, not hand-instrumented with explicit spans.

(2) test_resolved_log_level_prod_is_info mirrors the template's test_resolved_log_level_is_info_outside_dev (line 19) and exercises the real resolved_log_level property (settings.py lines 87-91) which returns INFO for non-dev environments. The docstring rationale ("never DEBUG, which would leak sensitive data") echoes the template's own intent comment at settings.py line 15-16. This is a domain-appropriate test-quality clean example: meaningful asserts on exact expected values for real, tracked config — not arbitrary code. batteries: [] is consistent with these being core (non-battery-gated) settings fields.

The clean code mirrors the template's actual React conventions rather than being arbitrary. It edits the real shipped file frontend/src/Items.tsx, extending its existing import line and appending after the existing Items() component. The DeleteButton uses the template's exact idioms: named-function component export (matching `export function Items()`/`App()` in Items.tsx and App.tsx, not arrow-const or default exports); useState(null) and boolean useState mirroring Items.tsx's `useState(null)` / `useState(false)`; error surfaced via `

` exactly like Items.tsx's `

Failed to load items.

` (and the role the Items.test.tsx asserts on); and a fetch with `if (!res.ok) throw new Error(...)` that copies api.ts's `if (!res.ok) throw new Error(...)` shape. Accessibility care (aria-busy) is consistent with the template's a11y-conscious style (aria-label, role=alert) — directly on-domain for the usability agent. One minor deviation: the template centralizes fetches in api.ts (fetchItems) keeping components fetch-free, whereas DeleteButton calls fetch inline; but it still uses api.ts's established fetch/res.ok/throw idiom, so this is idiomatic, not arbitrary. Net: a realistic, on-convention clean usability example.
